title: Nature of Sound Waves and Wave Modes
narrationScript: Every ultrasonic test starts with the same physical event. Something pushes on the atoms of a solid, and the atoms push on their neighbors. Sound is exactly that, a mechanical vibration handed from particle to particle through an elastic material. The particles themselves barely move. Each one oscillates a tiny distance around its rest position, shoves its neighbor, and settles back. What travels is the disturbance, not the material. And because sound is particle motion, it needs particles. It will cross steel, water, or air, but it will never cross a vacuum, and it crosses an air gap into metal so poorly that we bridge every probe with couplant. Frequency is simply how many times each particle swings back and forth every second. Human hearing runs from about twenty hertz to twenty thousand hertz. Anything above twenty thousand hertz is ultrasonic. Industrial testing lives far above that, from about half a megahertz up to twenty five megahertz, and most everyday work sits between one and ten megahertz. We climb that high for two reasons. Short wavelengths reflect from small discontinuities, the things we are paid to find, and high frequency beams stay narrow and directional, so we can aim them. Now, how does the vibration get into the part? Inside every probe is a thin piezoelectric element. Hit it with a sharp electrical pulse and it physically snaps thicker and thinner, hammering a short burst of vibration into whatever it touches. The same element works in reverse, turning returning vibration back into voltage for the receiver. But the probe face never mates perfectly with bare metal. There is always a microscopic air gap, and air transmits almost nothing into steel, so a film of couplant bridges the gap. Which wave mode develops in the part depends on how the energy enters. Drive the element flat against the surface and the particles are pushed straight in line with travel. That is a longitudinal wave. Tilt the beam with a plastic wedge and refraction plus mode conversion generate a shear wave. Tilt it even further and the energy hugs the surface as a surface wave. Send it into a plate only a few wavelengths thick and the whole wall flexes in plate waves. One element, four wave modes, all selected by geometry. Let's give each mode its character. The longitudinal wave, also called a compression wave, squeezes and stretches the material along the direction of travel, in alternating compressions and rarefactions. It is the fastest mode and the only one that travels through solids, liquids, and gases alike. In steel it moves at about five thousand nine hundred twenty meters per second. In aluminum, about six thousand three hundred twenty. In water, about one thousand four hundred eighty. In air, only about three hundred thirty. The shear wave moves its particles at right angles to the direction of travel, like a shaken rope. Sustaining that sideways motion takes a material rigid enough to resist a shearing slide, and liquids and gases are not, so shear waves exist only in solids. In steel they travel at about three thousand two hundred forty meters per second, almost exactly half the longitudinal speed. Half the speed means half the wavelength at the same frequency, which is finer sensitivity, and that is part of why angle beam weld testing runs on shear. The surface wave, called a Rayleigh wave, rolls its particles in little ellipses and concentrates its energy in a skin about one wavelength deep. It travels at roughly ninety percent of shear speed, about two thousand nine hundred meters per second in steel. It will follow a gentle curve all the way around a part, but a sharp corner or a surface breaking crack throws it back, which makes it a deliberate tool for finding surface cracks on shapes a straight beam cannot reach. It is also fragile. A smear of couplant or a fingertip resting on the part ahead of the probe soaks it up and drops the echo, and inspectors use that touch test to confirm what they are looking at. Finally, plate waves, called Lamb waves, appear when the wall thickness shrinks to about a wavelength. The entire plate vibrates, in symmetric or asymmetric patterns, and their velocity is not fixed. It changes with frequency, with thickness, and with the mode excited. Hold on to one rule above all. Velocity belongs to the material and the mode, never to the probe. Steel does not care what frequency you choose. Change the mode, though, and the speed, the wavelength, and the physics all change with it.
imagePrompt: A technician presses a straight-beam ultrasonic probe onto a thick steel forging in a fabrication shop while a portable flaw detector on a cart displays a bright waveform.

sections.heading: Sound Is Particle Motion
sections.body:
Sound is **mechanical vibration** passed from particle to particle through an elastic medium. Each atom oscillates a tiny distance around its rest position, shoves its neighbor, and settles back; the **energy travels, the particles do not**. Because the energy rides on particle-to-particle contact, sound needs a medium — it crosses steel, water, and air readily but cannot cross a vacuum, and it crosses an air gap into metal so poorly that couplant gets its own discussion two lessons from now.

Frequency is the number of complete vibration cycles per second:

- **Audible sound:** roughly 20 Hz to 20 kHz
- **Ultrasound:** anything above 20 kHz
- **Industrial UT:** about 0.5 to 25 MHz, with most production work between **1 and 10 MHz**

We work millions of cycles per second for two reasons. First, high frequency means short wavelength, and short wavelengths reflect from small discontinuities — the things we are paid to find. Second, high-frequency beams are directional: they can be aimed, steered with wedges, and focused, where audible sound simply floods the room. The price of high frequency — faster energy loss in coarse material — is the subject of the next lesson.

sections.heading: How Vibration Gets Into the Part
sections.body:
Inside every UT probe sits a thin **piezoelectric element**. A sharp electrical pulse makes the element snap thicker and thinner, hammering a short burst of vibration — a few cycles long — into whatever it touches; returning vibration squeezes the element and generates the voltage the receiver displays. Transducer construction gets its own lesson later in the course. The probe face never mates perfectly with bare metal: a microscopic **air gap** remains, and almost no energy crosses an air gap into steel, so a film of **couplant** — water, gel, glycerin, or oil — bridges it.

Which wave mode develops in the part is controlled by **how the energy enters**:

- **Straight in (normal incidence):** particles are driven in line with travel → **longitudinal** wave
- **Tilted entry on a wedge:** refraction and mode conversion generate a **shear** wave
- **Tilted past the second critical angle:** energy hugs the surface as a **surface (Rayleigh)** wave
- **Thin plate, suitable angle and frequency:** the whole wall flexes in **plate (Lamb)** modes

One element, four wave modes — selected by entry geometry, not by buying a different crystal. The angles that make this work are Snell's-law territory, covered in the final lesson of this module.

sections.caption: Longitudinal particles oscillate along the travel direction in compressions and rarefactions; shear particles oscillate across it, at roughly half the velocity.

sections.heading: Longitudinal and Shear Waves
sections.body:
The **longitudinal (compression) wave** drives its particles back and forth **parallel** to the direction of travel, stacking alternating zones of squeeze (compressions) and stretch (rarefactions). It is the fastest mode in any material and the only mode that propagates in **solids, liquids, and gases** alike — which is why immersion testing and couplant transmission are strictly longitudinal businesses. Typical longitudinal velocities: carbon steel **5,920 m/s** (0.233 in/µs), aluminum **6,320 m/s**, water **1,480 m/s**, air about **330 m/s**.

The **shear (transverse) wave** moves its particles **perpendicular** to travel, like a shaken rope. Sustaining that sideways motion requires a material rigid enough to resist shear — so **shear waves exist only in solids**; liquids and gases simply flow out of the way. Shear velocity in steel is about **3,240 m/s** (0.128 in/µs) and in aluminum 3,130 m/s — almost exactly **half the longitudinal velocity** in the same metal.

Half the velocity matters: at the same probe frequency a shear wave has **half the wavelength**, hence finer sensitivity, of its longitudinal cousin. Combine that with the steep refracted angles a wedge produces and you get the workhorse of weld inspection — the angle-beam shear wave — without ever changing the element.

sections.caption: Rayleigh waves roll along the surface about one wavelength deep; Lamb waves set a thin plate flexing in symmetric or asymmetric patterns.

sections.heading: Surface and Plate Waves
sections.body:
The **surface (Rayleigh) wave** travels along the skin of a part with particles orbiting in small **ellipses**, its energy concentrated within about **one wavelength** of the surface. It moves at roughly **90% of shear velocity** — about 2,900 m/s in steel. Two field behaviors define it:

- It **follows gentle contours** — around a radius, along a curved billet — with little loss, but a **sharp corner or a surface-breaking crack reflects it**, which makes it a deliberate tool for surface-crack detection on geometries a straight beam cannot cover.
- It lives at the surface, so the surface can kill it: excess couplant, dirt, or a **fingertip damped on the part ahead of the probe** absorbs the wave and drops the echo. Inspectors use that touch test to confirm which signal is the surface wave and where it is running.

**Plate (Lamb) waves** appear when wall thickness shrinks to the order of a wavelength. The whole plate vibrates as a unit, in **symmetric** (breathing) and **asymmetric** (flexing) mode families. Unlike the other modes, Lamb velocity is **dispersive** — it depends on frequency, thickness, and which mode is excited — so technique development works from dispersion data, not a single velocity table. They earn their keep scanning thin sheet, tubing walls, and bonded structure at long range.

sections.caption: The four wave modes at a glance — particle motion, speed, and where each earns its keep.

sections.title: Velocity belongs to the material and the mode
sections.body: The probe sets frequency; the **material and mode set velocity**. In steel the ordering is fixed: longitudinal ≈ **5,920 m/s**, shear ≈ **3,240 m/s** (about half), Rayleigh ≈ **2,900 m/s** (slightly under shear). Liquids and gases carry **no shear and no Rayleigh waves** — remember that every time a water path or a couplant film is part of your sound path.

interactive.title: Name That Mode
interactive.intro: Classify each statement under the wave mode it describes.

items.label: Particle motion is parallel to the direction of travel — compressions and rarefactions
items.why: Compression waves squeeze and stretch the material along the travel direction.

items.label: The only mode that can cross a liquid couplant film or immersion water path
items.why: Liquids support compression but not shear, so only longitudinal waves survive the crossing.

items.label: Particle motion is perpendicular to travel, and the mode exists only in solids
items.why: Shear needs rigidity; liquids and gases flow instead of springing back.

items.label: Travels at about half the longitudinal velocity in the same metal
items.why: Steel: about 3,240 m/s shear versus 5,920 m/s longitudinal.

items.label: The mode that standard 45, 60, and 70 degree weld wedges put into the steel
items.why: Wedges work between the critical angles, where only the shear wave remains in the part.

items.label: Particles orbit in ellipses, and the energy rides within about one wavelength of the surface
items.why: That shallow penetration is why Rayleigh waves excel at surface-breaking cracks.

items.label: Follows gentle contours but reflects from sharp corners — and a fingertip ahead of the probe damps it
items.why: Surface waves hug curvature, and anything resting on the surface absorbs them; the touch test exploits this.

items.label: Dispersive — velocity changes with frequency, wall thickness, and mode family
items.why: Lamb waves are whole-plate vibrations; symmetric and asymmetric modes each follow their own dispersion curves.

checkQuestions.question: In a longitudinal wave, particle motion is:
options: Perpendicular to the direction of propagation
Parallel to the direction of propagation
Elliptical and confined near the surface
Random in all directions
checkQuestions.explanation: Longitudinal (compression) waves drive particles back and forth along the travel direction, creating alternating compressions and rarefactions.

checkQuestions.question: A shear wave cannot travel through water because:
options: Water absorbs it completely as heat
Liquids cannot support the shear stress the mode requires
Its frequency is too high for liquids to follow
Water's impedance is too low for any wave mode
checkQuestions.explanation: Shear waves need a medium rigid enough to resist sideways sliding. Liquids and gases flow rather than spring back, so only longitudinal waves cross them — water does carry longitudinal sound.

checkQuestions.question: Longitudinal velocity in a steel part is 5,920 m/s. The shear velocity in the same part is closest to:
checkQuestions.explanation: Shear velocity in a metal is roughly half the longitudinal velocity — about 3,240 m/s in steel. The 2,900 m/s distractor is the Rayleigh (surface) wave velocity.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement about Rayleigh (surface) waves is correct?
options: They penetrate several inches deep regardless of wavelength
They follow gentle contours and reflect from sharp corners and surface-breaking cracks
They travel faster than longitudinal waves in the same material
They propagate undisturbed across pooled couplant on the surface
checkQuestions.explanation: Rayleigh waves concentrate energy within about one wavelength of the surface, follow gentle curvature, and are reflected by sharp corners and surface cracks; anything sitting on the surface — including pooled couplant — damps them.

checkQuestions.question: Lamb (plate) waves become practical when:
options: The part is at least ten wavelengths thick
The wall thickness is on the order of a wavelength — and velocity then depends on frequency, thickness, and mode
The test material is a liquid
The frequency drops below 20 kHz
checkQuestions.explanation: Lamb waves are whole-plate vibrations that exist in thin sections. They are dispersive: velocity varies with the frequency-thickness combination and the symmetric or asymmetric mode excited.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Basic UT Course §2.1 (nature of sound waves)
ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Basic UT Course §2.2 (modes of sound-wave generation)
Reference velocities: steel 5,920 / 3,240 m/s; aluminum 6,320 / 3,130 m/s; water 1,480 m/s; air 330 m/s
